a11y.guideline_desc: Consider assistive technologies and diverse viewing environments.

token.bp_desc: WIM UI supports responsive design. The layout switches based on the following breakpoints.
token.bp_down_desc: Used to apply styles only below (max-width) the specified breakpoint.
token.bp_scss_desc: These tokens are defined as a map variable `$breakpoints` in `src/tokens/_breakpoints.scss`, and mixins to easily write media queries are provided.
token.bp_sm_device: Large smartphones or small tablets in portrait mode
token.bp_up_desc: Used in a 'mobile-first' layout to apply styles above (min-width) the specified breakpoint.
token.bp_mq_vs_cq_desc: WIM UI uses both media queries and container queries depending on the context. The key question is: does the component respond to the viewport width, or to the width of the container it is placed in?
token.bp_mq_criterion: The component itself defines page structure, or always occupies the full viewport width
token.bp_cq_criterion: The component is placed inside a layout and does not know how wide its container will be
token.bp_mq_examples: AppShell (sidebar show/hide), Dialog, BottomSheet (rendered in portal), global spacing scale, device feature detection (prefers-color-scheme, prefers-reduced-motion)
token.bp_cq_examples: QueryBuilder, BentoGrid, and other UI components that can be placed anywhere in a layout
token.bp_cq_why_title: Why container queries for UI components?
token.bp_cq_why_desc: When a component like QueryBuilder is used in a main area beside a sidebar, the viewport may be 1200px wide while the actual available width is only 600px. A media query at 768px would never trigger, causing the layout to overflow. Container queries respond to the actual rendered width of the component's container, making Storybook viewport tests accurately reflect real-world usage.
token.bp_cq_usage_desc: Add container-type: inline-size to the component's root element. Then use @container rules to target its descendants.
token.bp_cq_note: Container queries are supported in all modern browsers (Chrome 105+, Safari 16+, Firefox 110+).
token.color_checker_desc: Checks the contrast ratio between background and text colors to ensure accessibility standards are met.
token.color_ct_desc: Colors for text or icons placed on semantic color backgrounds.
token.type_alias_desc: To apply consistent styling across components, aliases with the `--wim-` prefix are provided for the global tokens above. It is recommended to use these direct alias variables when developing or customizing WIM UI components.
token.type_desc1: Defines font sizes, weights, and line heights for consistent typography. Font sizes follow a modular scale with a 1.125 (Major Second) ratio.
token.type_desc2: The scale ratio is managed by the `$font-scale-ratio` variable in `src/tokens/_spacings.scss`. Changing this value adjusts the font ratio across the system.
token.type_ff_desc: Font specifications for each language used throughout the application. The appropriate font is automatically applied according to the `lang` attribute of the html/body tag.
token.type_lh_jpan_loose:
Loose (1.8)
Easy to read in Japanese text, especially on mobile devices and long body text. Gives a relaxed impression and makes eye movement smooth.
token.type_lh_jpan_normal:
Normal (1.6)
The standard line height for WIM UI. Offers the best balance between readability and information density.
token.type_lh_jpan_tight:
Tight (1.4)
Used when you want to increase information density, such as headings and labels.
token.type_lh_latn_loose:
Loose (1.6)
The loose line height for Latin scripts provides ample breathing room between lines. It is ideal for legibility in long-form content.
token.type_lh_latn_normal:
Normal (1.4)
Normal line height is the standard for most application UI elements. It offers a balanced density that works well for descriptions.
token.type_lh_latn_tight:
Tight (1.2)
Tight line height is specifically designed for headings. It maintains strong visual proximity for related text elements.
token.type_text_ex_large:
Extra Large Text (20.25px)
Uses the 1.125 squared ratio ($font-size-2).
token.type_text_ex_small:
Extra Small Text (12.64px)
Uses the 1.125 power of -2 ratio ($font-size-minus-2).
token.type_text_small:
Small Text (14.22px)
Uses the 1.125 power of -1 ratio ($font-size-minus-1).
token.overview_desc: This is a list of design tokens that form the foundation of style definitions for WIM UI. It is recommended to use these variables to maintain a consistent design.

welcome: Welcome to the WIM UI Design System

doc.inputBase_long_desc: Internal component that serves as the foundation for all text-based input components.
doc.inputBase_usage_desc: Mainly used to build other components. It handles the 'input container' styling including borders, backgrounds, and icon positioning.
doc.inputBase_design_intent: InputBase provides a consistent UI for input fields, managing layouts for icons, clear buttons, and semantic states.
doc.inputBase_choice_matrix_desc: Decide when to use InputBase versus FieldTemplate.
doc.inputBase_choice_use_when: When you need to create a custom input field (like Search or Select) that needs identical styling for its border and icons.
doc.inputBase_choice_alt_when: When you need to wrap an already existing input component with a label and error message.
doc.inputBase_anatomy_root_desc: The main wrapper that defines borders and background.
doc.inputBase_anatomy_content_desc: Space for the actual input or selected value.
doc.inputBase_content_guideline_1: Keep icon usage minimal to avoid clutter.
doc.inputBase_content_guideline_2: Ensure focus rings are clearly visible.
doc.inputBase_motion_desc: Subtle transitions on border color and clear button visibility.
doc.inputBase_a11y_desc: Ensures interactive icons have correct roles and labels.
doc.inputBase_scenario_1: Custom search boxes with action icons.
doc.inputBase_scenario_2: Complex input wrappers for specialized data entry.
doc.inputBase_best_practice_1: Use semantic colors for validation states.
doc.inputBase_best_practice_2: Always provide clear labels externally (via FieldTemplate).
doc.fieldTemplate_long_desc: A wrapper component that adds labels, error messages, and required indicators to any input field.
doc.fieldTemplate_usage_desc: Wraps any form component to provide a standardized label and error handling layout.
doc.fieldTemplate_design_intent: Consistency in form layouts by standardizing how labels and errors are displayed.
doc.fieldTemplate_choice_use_when: For almost all user-facing form fields.
doc.fieldTemplate_choice_alt_when: For internal controls or dense UI where labels are unnecessary.
doc.fieldTemplate_motion_desc: Error messages fade in to grab attention.
doc.fieldTemplate_a11y_desc: Connects labels to inputs via IDs and errors via aria-describedby.
doc.fieldTemplate_scenario_2: Settings pages with vertical or horizontal layouts.
doc.fieldTemplate_best_practice_1: Use horizontal layout for dense desktop forms.

dashboard.intro: You can check the development status and specification overview of all WIM UI components.
dashboard.tokens_link: For design foundations (colors, typography, etc.), please refer to the Design Token Overview.

guide.numeric_std_desc: Guidelines and technical considerations for consistent numeric input handling across browsers.
guide.numeric_std_concept_desc: While <input type="number"> seems ideal for numeric input, its cross-browser behavior is inconsistent, particularly regarding non-numeric characters like 'e' (scientific notation) and local formatting. We aim to standardize this behavior.
guide.numeric_std_usage_warning_desc: Use type="text" with inputmode="numeric" for fixed-format numbers (credit cards, IDs).
guide.numeric_std_usage_do_1: Use step and pattern attributes to guide user input.
guide.numeric_std_usage_dont_1: Don't rely on browser-native spin buttons for precise control.
guide.numeric_std_usage_dont_2: Avoid using type="number" when non-numeric separators (hyphens, spaces) are allowed.
guide.numeric_std_usage_dont_3: Do not assume the value is always a valid number on 'input' event across all browsers.
guide.numeric_std_problem_1_desc: Different browsers handle invalid characters differently when in number mode.
guide.numeric_std_problem_2_desc: The 'e' character is often allowed but unexpected by users.
guide.numeric_std_safari_behavior: Allows 'e' and handles as scientific notation
guide.numeric_std_problem_3_desc: Native spin buttons and padding vary wildly between rendering engines.
guide.numeric_std_problem_4_desc: Some screen readers treat number inputs as spin buttons, which might not be desirable for all types of data.
guide.numeric_std_spin_button_warning: Consider hiding native spin buttons via CSS for a more consistent UI.
guide.numeric_std_recommend_desc: For most use cases involving IDs or formatted numbers, use type="text" with appropriate semantic hints.
guide.numeric_std_exp_notation_desc: Be aware that large numbers may still be converted to scientific notation in some contexts.
guide.action_std_desc: Unified sizing and loading states for all action-oriented components.
guide.action_std_concept_desc: To ensure a consistent user experience, all buttons and interactive elements must follow the same sizing scale (sm, md, lg) and handle loading states predictably.
guide.action_std_rule_1_desc: All buttons must use the sm, md, lg scale defined in the design tokens.
guide.action_std_rule_2_desc: Interactive elements should show a spinner or skeleton when performing async operations.
guide.action_std_rule_3_desc: Icons always maintain a 8px gap from the text label.
guide.action_std_rule_4_desc: All interactive elements must have a visible 2px outline on focus.
guide.action_std_note_desc: Legacy components are being phased out in favor of the new standardized versions listed above.
